results.title: A Place in history: Whitehall
results.brief_description: Whitehall, connecting Trafalgar Square with the Houses of Parliament, is a street steeped in tradition. Horse Guards Parade, the Cenotaph, the Foreign Office, the Admiralty and at either end Big Ben and the Whitehall Theatre are its landmarks. Lord Mancroft tells the story of this famous street down which he has so often walked during his distinguished career as politician and statesman.

results.title: A place in history : the temples
results.brief_description: “Summary: the inner and middle temples form an oasis of quiet in the heart of London. this is the centre for the legal profession. the two inns have occupied the temple area for over 600 years, and one of the most famous members, Jeremy Thorpe takes us on a tour and interviews people associated with the inns”

results.title: A Place for pottery
results.creator_credit: Douglas Cox and Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology
results.brief_description: The art of pottery from the quarrying of the raw material at Campbellfield, Victoria, to the finished article. Shows the clay being mixed, wedged to remove air bubbles, the potter at work, drying, firing, and glazing. Displays pottery in both its functional and decorative capacities.

results.title: A Place for Aunt Lois
results.creator_credit: Gene Feldman and Wombat Productions, Inc.
results.brief_description: Tells the story of a nin year old girl and her aunt, a young, childless woman, whose marriage has ended. Kathy thinking that Aunt Lois must be lonely without a husband and children, attempts to introduce her to Bill, the handsome man who operates the rides at the amusement park. But Aunt Lois is a woman who stands at the beginning of a new life, and who accepts herself as an autonomous individual, quite capable of living that life fully. Eventually Kathy comes to the conclusion that it does not matter whether her aunt is married or not, whether she has children or not, whether she is the perfect physical idea of the time or not. What does matter is the kind of person Lois is.

results.title: A Pioneer stud
results.brief_description: One of the oldest established sheep studs in Australia provides the setting for this film; the stud has been in existence since 1827. The daily routine on this stud farm is studied in detail.

results.title: A Pint of milk
results.creator_credit: Malcolm Otton, Stanley Hawes and Australian National Film Board
results.brief_description: One of a series of films designed for use in junior social studies classes. Shows how city people get their milk. Opens with scenes on a dairy farm- bringing in the herd, and milking the cows. The milk is taken to a depot, where it is tested for cleanliness, then sent by rail to the city. Here it is pasteurised and bottled then taken by the milk van to be delivered to homes in the city.

results.title: A Picture for Harold's room
results.creator_credit: Gene Deitch, Morton Schindel and Weston Woods Studios
results.brief_description: Adapted from the book written and illustrated by Crockett Johnson, this animated story tells about a little boy, Harold, who uses a purple crayon to create various adventures in which he alone participates.

results.title: A Phantasy
results.brief_description: A surrealist abstract art film with pastel drawings and cut-out animation by Norman McLaren and music for saxophone and synthetic sound by Maurice Blackburn. In a dream-like landscape, inanimate but familiar objects come to life to disport themselves in grave dances and playful rituals. Music by Maurice Blackburn. (Awards: Venice, Toronto, Boston)

results.title: A Personal history of the Australian surf: being the confessions of a straight poofter
results.creator_credit: Michael Blakemore, Jeremy Cornford, John B Murray, Phillip Adams and Adams Packer Films
results.brief_description: Australian theatre and film director, Michael Blakemore, recreates his childhood and adolescence in the 1930s and 1940s in Sydney and reflects on the events which led to his career in the theatre and eventually a directorship of Britain’s National Theatre. By implication the film is also something of a mini history of surfing in Australia. Utilising a compilation of old newsreels, family snapshots, re-enactments and Blakemore’s commentary, the film is an intriguing piece of Australian social history.

results.title: A Peep into a well known pottery works
results.brief_description: A stack of clay, flint and stone are taken to the Mill house to be ground. Then to the strip house for weighing of the ingredients, sieving, and plugging the clay. Often the clay is passed to the potters for throwing and turning. The product is then dried and placed into an oven. This is followed by painting and glazing. Other items are passed to the gilders where only the best gold is used, and then to the burnishing shop. A museum contains many specimens up to the present day.
